title: how to sell $5,000 Claude Code AIOS to SMBs (full breakdown)
summary: Liam Ottley and Tyler Nelson outline a bottom-up playbook for AI agencies to build and sell AI operating systems (AIOS) to small and medium-sized businesses. The approach involves creating a Context OS that integrates chatbot history and external tools like CRM and analytics, layering automations and agents, and implementing ROI tracking before productizing as turnkey solutions or managed services.
writeup: The speaker argues that AI agency work is undergoing a structural inversion: the dominant model of auditing a business, identifying point solutions, and building standalone agents into specific data sources is being replaced by a bottom-up approach that starts with an AI operating system (AIOS). The AIOS layers a context OS (exported ChatGPT or Claude history baked into a standardized workspace) with platform integrations (Stripe, CRM, ad platforms, analytics) and slash-command workflows, so a coding agent already knows the business before any automation is defined. This shift compresses the path from discovery to working automation and reframes what AI agencies sell, moving from bespoke builds toward contextual infrastructure and training.
key_points: The prior AI agency model centered on a top-down sequence: audit the business, map point solutions, build standalone agents hooked into individual data sources; the speaker contends this approach has been doing things the hard way and will be largely superseded.

The replacement is a bottom-up AIOS stack built in Claude Code or Cursor, with three layers: a context OS seeded from exported ChatGPT and Claude conversation history, platform integrations (Stripe, CRM, Facebook Ads, Google Analytics) that support both read and write operations, and slash-command workflows such as /explore that guide founders through automation discovery and implementation planning.

The speaker runs his own business entirely inside Claude Code with all integrations connected, no longer using the native SaaS interfaces at all, which he cites as direct evidence that the AIOS model works at the operational level and carries significant implications for B2B SaaS.

Once context and integrations are in place, the pathway to automation contracts sharply: the /explore command walks a founder through multiple phases of identifying what to automate, producing a technical implementation plan, chunking it, and testing it, without requiring deep technical knowledge on the founder's part.

For agencies, the delivery spectrum runs from training offers (teaching digitally native founders to operate the AIOS themselves) to in-person installs where the agency owner sits with a founder, builds out context and integrations during the setup engagement, and solves a first concrete problem as part of the fee.

ROI tracking becomes a consequential downstream problem once AIOS is deployed: agencies need to instrument which automations fired, how often, and how long the equivalent manual task would have taken, in order to quantify and communicate the value delivered.
